analysis.note: Masculine nominative singular in a nominal predication with vikalpaḥ; rendered as the technical expression “dependent nature.”

verification.detail: compound stem paratantrasvabhāva as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Masculine nominative singular; the nominal predicate equated with paratantrasvabhāvaḥ.
Masculine nominative singular adjective qualifying vikalpaḥ; translated as “arisen from conditions/grounds.”

verification.detail: compound stem pratyayodbhava as Pum/Prathama/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Past passive participial adjective from niṣ-√pad, used substantively with implied svabhāvaḥ: “the perfected [nature].”
Genitive singular; in this grammar-only run its most natural antecedent is the preceding dependent nature/construction, but the exact reference is context-sensitive.
Instrumental singular used with rahita/rahitatā: “devoid of the former.”
Feminine abstract noun in -tā from rahita, “absence, being devoid”; yā agrees with it.
Second contrastive/transition particle, introducing the niṣpanna definition.
Relative pronoun agreeing with rahitatā: “which”; the correlative predication is niṣpannaḥ [svabhāvaḥ].

justifications.decision: The two masculine nominative singulars paratantrasvabhāvaḥ and vikalpaḥ form a nominal sentence. Since paratantra is an adjective meaning dependent and svabhāva is a masculine noun meaning nature/state of being, the simplest compound construal is karmadhāraya: “dependent nature.”
options_considered: loose apposition: “dependent, the nature, is construction”
justifications.chosen: paratantrasvabhāvaḥ = “the dependent nature,” a karmadhāraya compound equated with vikalpaḥ.

evidence.translation: Grammar-only evidence: nominative singular compound plus nominative singular vikalpaḥ gives a nominal predication, “the dependent nature is construction/imagination.”

justifications.decision: The compound is construed as a tatpuruṣa with udbhava as the head, “origin/arising,” and pratyaya as the source or conditioning basis. Because it qualifies vikalpaḥ, the verse says that construction is condition-arisen.

evidence.translation: Grammar-only evidence: masculine nominative singular pratyayodbhavaḥ agrees with vikalpaḥ; udbhava supplies the semantic head “arising/origin.”

justifications.decision: niṣpannaḥ cannot directly qualify rahitatā, which is feminine. It is therefore best taken with an implied masculine svabhāvaḥ carried over from paratantrasvabhāvaḥ; the following feminine relative yā introduces the rahitatā that defines this perfected nature.
justifications.chosen: niṣpannaḥ is an elliptical masculine nominative: “the perfected [nature].”

evidence.quote: niṣpannas tasya pūrveṇa sadā rahitatā tu yā
evidence.translation: Grammar-only evidence: niṣpannaḥ is masculine nominative singular, while rahitatā and yā are feminine nominative singular; the gender mismatch points to an implied svabhāvaḥ.

justifications.decision: rahita is regularly construed with an instrumental complement for what is absent or lacking; pūrveṇa is therefore taken with rahitatā, not as an adverb “formerly” or as an agent. tasya is a genitive dependent on rahitatā: “the absence/being-devoid of it,” idiomatically “in it.”
options_considered: instrumental complement of rahitatā: “devoid of the former”
justifications.chosen: tasya pūrveṇa sadā rahitatā = “its always being devoid of the former.”

evidence.translation: Grammar-only evidence: pūrveṇa is instrumental singular, matching the complement expected with rahita/rahitatā; sadā adverbially modifies the state of absence.

justifications.decision: rahitatā is an abstract feminine noun in -tā from rahita. The following yā is the relative pronoun agreeing with it; tā is not an independent word here.
options_considered: yā agreeing with an implied feminine noun other than rahitatā
justifications.chosen: rahitatā tu yā is one abstract noun plus a relative pronoun, “which absence/being-devoid.”

evidence.translation: Grammar-only evidence: yā is feminine nominative singular and agrees with rahitatā; the internal -tā is the abstract suffix.

translation:
But the dependent nature (paratantra-svabhāva) is construction (vikalpa), arisen from conditions (pratyaya);
and the perfected [nature] (niṣpanna) is that ever-being-without the former in it.
one_shot_delta: Without commentary, tasya can only be assigned by syntax and context; its exact antecedent may be taken as vikalpaḥ or paratantrasvabhāvaḥ.
Without commentary or the preceding verse, pūrveṇa might be misread as “by the former [dependent nature]” rather than as “devoid of the former [nature].”

A dictionary-only rendering of pratyaya could drift toward “belief/conception”; the compound-context with udbhava supports the broader “condition/ground,” but the technical sense is not proved by commentary here.
The sequence rahita_tā in the analyzer could tempt a wrong split; grammatically it is the abstract noun rahitatā, “absence/being-devoid.”
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 presents paratantra_svabhāvaḥ and pratyaya_udbhavaḥ as segmented compound member plus head; I record each as a single inflected compound word, as required for verse-word analysis.
ByT5 gives niṣpannaḥ with lemma/root niṣpad; I use the nominal stem niṣpanna for the subanta analysis, while noting its derivation from niṣ-√pad.
ByT5 splits rahita_tā; I analyze rahitatā as one feminine abstract noun in -tā, not as rahita plus an independent word tā.
open_questions: The precise antecedent of tasya is not finally fixed from this verse alone in a commentary-free run.
The identity of pūrva, “the former,” depends on prior context; from this verse alone it can only be translated generically as “the former.”
